stages.heading: Stardate 2400.0 (Introduction)
commentary: Welcome to the continuation of the AP Bot solitaire adventures. We were decisively successful against the previous opponent, so we will be bumping it up a notch. We also will be adding some complexity back into the equation. Furthermore, we have augmented our Replay Center to support AGT-style Heavy Terrain. Thus, as part of field testing, we will be hand-picking this scenario card; instead of potentially using the appropriate Shuffler to draw 2 and applying their effects if we were to play with them.

Since we are playing as the Replicators, we will set this up as if it was 30 CP homeworlds; so Apollo starts with 7 home system economic rolls. Normal difficulty gives them no handicap in either direction (+0 rolls). Our faction choice causes what would normally be a home system barren planet to be non-barren for the other players, allowing them to colonize with Terraforming 0 (+1 roll) and starting with 10 additional CP in the fleet bank. We then flip a coin to determine who would go first, once they launch their first ships. Tails, Apollo gains the initiative!

On top of all of this, this would normally be the map size (4 or more deep space rows) where an AP Bot has the goal of colonizing one or more deep space planets, the condition for buying Exploration 1 right away. Since we are playing as the Replicators, deep space colonies and Terraforming technology are shifted as if the map was one tier smaller... which knocks the goal back down to 0, simplifying some of their strategies.

stages.heading: Stardate 2400.3
commentary: We take advantage of the simplified opening, and already uncover our home system supernova.

stages.heading: Stardate 2400.6
commentary: Since we are playing as the Replicators this time, we can choose when to colonize a planet. Sometimes it is advantageous to wait until later in a trio of turns.

stages.heading: Stardate 2400.9
commentary: Almost done with this first trio...

stages.heading: Stardate 2401.0
commentary: We have now made it to the first economic phase. Nonetheless, as important as expansion is, we opted to save up for an RP. 30 CP is a lot of money to spend this way, and home system minerals only give us 65 CP.

stages.heading: Stardate 2401.2
commentary: If we encounter a planet on the first turn in a trio...

stages.heading: Stardate 2401.5
commentary: ... we can opt to go a little further.

stages.heading: Stardate 2401.8
commentary: If appropriate, we can then backtrack to the planets we initially uncovered.

stages.heading: Stardate 2402.0
commentary: So far, we have been finding planets more than minerals. Useful in its own way, at least.

stages.heading: Stardate 2402.1
commentary: Probably the most tranquil opening so far, when there are very few ships to move, and extra <q>actions</q> to perform.

stages.heading: Stardate 2402.4
commentary: Then again, Apollo's opening has been vastly simplified.

stages.heading: Stardate 2402.7
commentary: ... while also simulating a rudimentary fog of war.

stages.heading: Stardate 2403.0
commentary: Our closest colonies have now reached full strength. Starting with the next economic phase, they will begin producing ships locally.

stages.heading: Stardate 2403.3
commentary: It will be a bit before we can resume exploring the rest of our home systems.

stages.heading: Stardate 2403.6
commentary: Such is a side effect of not building extra hulls, and also placing our homeworld close to a corner.

stages.heading: Stardate 2403.9
commentary: Especially when they continue to give us planets (of which now 2 remain unaccounted for), when 40 CP worth of mienrals remain hidden...

stages.heading: Stardate 2404.0
commentary: And now our colonies have produced us a handful of hulls. Meanwhile, if we are understanding recent revelations correctly, the AP Bot places its Exploration Vessels this economic phase. The first EV is placed at the center, a second EV placed at a random hex adjacent to deep space.

stages.heading: Stardate 2404.2
commentary: And with that in mind, the AI is already out into deep space. To think we may have unwittingly complicated their adventures when we opted to play as the Replicators... Both of their EVs are already destroyed! (But, luckily for them, doomsday machines are still disabled.)

stages.heading: Stardate 2404.5
commentary: What an embarassing series of events. The first two deep space hexes already wipe out their EVs!

stages.heading: Stardate 2404.8
commentary: The question becomes; how much are we willing to risk to deep space? Exploration technology still has not yet been unlocked for purchase!
The prerequisite for Replicator Exploration is uncovering all of the home systems or being invaded by an enemy player.

stages.heading: Stardate 2405.0
commentary: During our economic phase, we buy our first RP. Expensive (-30), but worth it.
{prodTable}{replicatorTable}
The AI finally activates the rest of the way. Its home system rolls for this phase are 1,1,2,3,7,7,9,10. Paranoia Checks are then rolled as follows:
1. Exploration: 28 (pass) → Skip this technology
• Bidding: 91 (pass) → Set aside 0 CP

Since the goal of deep space colonies is at zero, they must roll for fleet launch. A 2 was rolled, so they launch an expansion fleet anyway. Movement 2 is bought right away (-20). A 7 is then rolled on the extra Movement step (skip >5). That concludes this economic phase.

stages.heading: Stardate 2405.1
commentary: The lack of exploration significantly limits where their expansion fleet can go.

stages.heading: Stardate 2405.4
commentary: (Not all hope is lost. Two fleets can merge if one or both of them are unidentified. A fleet that reaches or exceeds 120 CP this way will become an extermination fleet.)

stages.heading: Stardate 2405.7 (The Battle for Ada)
commentary: Apollo's initial fleet can now battle the NPA planet of Ada. As it is the very first battle, they roll for a new fleet strategy. A 99, which normally would be the Yarr strategy. But; because replicators are present on this map, it is outright harmful to their cause (we are immune to their boarding ships). Next up, they rolled an 84 (Scout Rush). An AP bot would not use this strategy against NPA planets, so it must reroll this result also.

Finally, a 12 was rolled (Biggest Ship), a valid strategy for all battles. Since it is battling an NPA planet, it skips the pre-emptive purchase of counter tech. Its compatible CP is then spent as follows:
1. Ship Size 2 (120 → 110 tech CP)
• Ship Size 3 (110 → 95 tech CP)

• A 7 was rolled on the upgrade step, which attempted to buy Tactics. Since neither Attack nor Defense have been bought previously, we skip
  • Attack 1 (95 → 75 tech CP)
• Ship Size 4 (75 → 55 tech CP)
• Defense 1 (55 → 35 tech CP)
• Ship Size 5 (35 → 15 tech CP)
• Tactics 1 (15 → 0 tech CP)
• Battleship x5 (100 → 0 assigned CP)
Afterwards, the battle is conducted as follows:
1. Round 1: Ada's Alien-A rolls a 5 (≤2 to hit)
• Apollo's battleships rolled 10,2/1/5/1 (4 hits ≤5). Using an order similar (but not identical) to Replicator Solitaire, these hits damage their Alien-A to 1 HP, while destroying the rest

• Round 2: Alien-A rolls a 6 (≤2 to hit)
  • Apollo's battleships now benefit from Fleet Size Bonus, and rolled 1,2,6,8,10 (3 hits ≤6), finishing off the last of the NPA ships. The planet is now subdued
Now that the battle is over, and that we are forming our own fleet; we will start cleaning up the board a bit.

stages.heading: Stardate 2406.0
commentary: 4 rows of deep space is barely tolerable with no exploration. With Heavy Terrain in effect, it makes these counters all the more dangerous. Thus, we will go on ahead and buy this technology (-15).
{prodTable}{replicatorTable}

Apollo now gets a chance to conduct its economics. Its home system rolls for this phase are 1,1,4,4,7,7,8. The only paranoia check that makes sense at this point is bidding, for which a 17 was rolled (pass).
They roll a 3 for fleet launch. Even with a +1 maintenance penalty, they launch another expansion fleet. They do not have enough funds to further upgrade Movement, so they end it here.

stages.heading: Stardate 2406.3
commentary: Apollo, being stalled by the inability to uncover new grou— err, space, with just 2 expansion fleets, uses the prime directive to combine their fleets. We use this opportunity to combine 2 Type 0s into the newly unlocked Type Exp.

stages.heading: Stardate 2406.6
commentary: Apollo merges their two fleets. Now that the combined total reaches/exceeds 120 CP, the newest fleet has now become an extermination fleet (100 CP known + 60 CP unknown = 160 CP total). Unlike an expansion fleet, these fleets will spend ships to uncover deep space.

We have found our very first space station. These new craft are accurate, and somewhat hard to hurt. Despite how valuable this hex could be (20 CP total); we will keep it around as an obstacle, and not engage it.

stages.heading: Stardate 2406.9
commentary: It will be a little longer before they begin uncovering a path. Two space stations back to back is quite clumpy. Again, we will leave them both as obstacles. (Technically, we are not supposed to reveal pirates, but we will leave it face up as a reference.)

stages.heading: Stardate 2407.0
commentary: Our Movement systems are due for an upgrade soon. We need only to hold on for a little longer.
{prodTable}{replicatorTable}

Apollo was able to merge two fleets together, so it still pays maintenance with just 1 home system roll. Its remaining rolls for this phase are 1,3,3,6,7,7,8. First, a paranoia check is made on Exploration, having rolled a 68 (pass). Next up is bidding, having rolled a 13 (pass).
For fleet launch, they rolled a 7. With a +1 maintenance penalty still, no launch takes place this phase. The AP bot instead builds a Forward Operating Base (-20) close to deep space.

stages.heading: Stardate 2407.2
commentary: Now that Apollo's extermination fleet is in position to uncover a path, they will do just that. They uncover the next hex in their path as if they had Exploration 1. But, in return for not having actually developed the technology, they sacrifice 6 CP (1 scout worth) in their unidentified fleet to unexplored space. The rest of their fleet remains stationary.

stages.heading: Stardate 2407.5
commentary: The first hex they uncovered this way was impassable, so they must uncover another hex. Another 6 CP was sacrificed.

stages.heading: Stardate 2407.8
commentary: The second hex they uncovered is a fold in space; very useful to them. They can uncover the next hex in their newest path right away! (Which means sacrificing another 6 CP.)
Meanwhile, we decided we would try to snag the dense minerals that are near their black hole. Before we can claim them though, we must roll for survival. A 10 was rolled, sucking in our Type 0...

stages.heading: Stardate 2408.0
commentary: Movement systems have been upgraded naturally. Our hull count continues to grow nicely.
{prodTable}{replicatorTable}

Apollo has quite a scary fleet, but each hex it has to uncover the hard way weakens it a little. Still, its home system rolls are 2,2,3,4,5,6,9. First, a paranoia check is made on Exploration, having rolled a 51 (pass). Next up is bidding, having rolled a 14 (pass).

For fleet launch, they rolled a 6. They can barely launch a fresh fleet... They were able to save up enough money to buy Movement 3, at least. They also rolled a 25 on their paranoia check, allowing them to place their fleet at their FOB.

stages.heading: Stardate 2408.1
commentary: Since entry into black holes is entirely forbidden for an extermination fleet, they must uncover yet another deep space hex. As they do not have the required funds to hire the pirate they just uncovered, the pirate simply disappears.
We found an extremely valuable hex. Dense minerals (10 CP worth) and a space wreck! We claim them both right away, and reposition our fleet of Type 0s!!

stages.heading: Stardate 2408.4
commentary: We take advantage of our nearest colony to their fleet, and transform all of our Type 0s already in position into Type IIs.

stages.heading: Stardate 2408.7A
commentary: Apollo now takes advantage of the path they just exposed to our home systems. Since we previously revealed both counters (unwittingly to their advantage), they can simply hop on in and battle the space station.

36 CP worth of unidentified ships remain, and their tech bank is insufficient to buy any more. Therefore, they build one additional Battleship and 1 Battlecruiser, crediting a leftover 1 CP back to the fleet bank. Since the AP bot has a Tactics advantage, its battleships go first, having rolled 2,2,6,6,9,10 (2 hits ≤5). The space station has been easily dispatched.

stages.heading: Stardate 2408.7P
commentary: We take advantage of the many opportunities thst are available, learning both from the NPA and from Apollo. The NPAs rolled 2,3,7 (2 hits ≤6) and Apollo rolled 2,5,6,7,7,8 (5 hits ≤7), easily dispatching both older Type 0s.

stages.heading: Stardate 2409.0
commentary: The surge of RP allows us to skip directly to Type IXs and Type XIs! But, we are nearing depletion. Since we do not need to buy any more RP, we will instead bid 6 CP (to try to steal the initiative) and buy an extra homeworld hull. This allows us to build a Type XI right away.
{prodTable}{replicatorTable}

Apollo's military grows, but the maintenance is also eating away a quarter of its home system income. Since we can not really use alien technology cards, we opt to simplify their end and simply treat them as if they cashed in a deep space mineral. Its remaining rolls are 1,3,4,6,9,9,10. The only aspect where it makes sense to conduct a paranoia check is Exploration; having rolled a 13 (pass). Their extermination fleet is <q>threatened</q>, so they bid right away. 7,9 were rolled. 3 is subtracted from the lowest (fixed), and 1 more is added back (paranoia tier). They bid 5 CP... which barely succumbs to our 6.

For fleet launch, they rolled a 1. They easily launch a fresh fleet... but their fleet bank is insuffucient to meet the quota of 60, so funds were drawn from the tech bank to replace the shortage... leaving 11 CP leftover in the tech bank, not really enough to buy more technology. Finally, they rolled a 6 on their paranoia check, barely allowing them to place their fleet at their FOB.

stages.heading: Stardate 2409.3
commentary: By stealing the initiative, we can get our ships out of harm's way, and just let their fleet bombard our forward colony. They can not work on Anti-Replicator transports if they have not the money to do so. Still, their battleships rolled 1,4,5,8,9,10 (3 hits ≤6), reducing our forward colony to minimal strength.

stages.heading: Stardate 2409.6P (The Battle for Epsilon)
commentary: We pile all ships able to counter-battle their extermination fleet. That is 2 Type 0s (that we did not have time to refit), 3 Type IXs, and 13 Type XIs. Since their fleet is already known, we go straight to battle.
1. Round 1 Their Battleships have the highest weapon class, so they shoot first. They rolled 6,10,6 against our Type 0s (2 hits ≤6), and 4,8,9 against our Type IXs (1 hit ≤5)
• Their Battlecruiser has the tactics advantage, so it is next. It rolled an 8 against our Type IXs (≤5 to hit)

• Our Type XIs have now gotten into position. With the assistance of FSB, and focusing on their battlecruiser first, we rolled 5,9,7 (2 hits ≤7). The rest of the rolls are then spent accordingly, having rolled 1,2,3,3,4,4,5,5,7,9 (8 hits ≤6). 2 battleships go down, and a third is down to 1 HP
• Our remaining Type IXs have rolled 2,8 (1 hit ≤4), finishing off another battleship

• Round 2: Before their remaining 2 Battleships make a decision, they make a morale check. The losses are so immense, that their threshold is a staggering 91. A 46 was rolled, which causes the remaining ships to retreat

stages.heading: Stardate 2409.6A (The Battle for Epsilon)
commentary: The surviving battleships take advantage of the reinforcements in the retreat hex, merging them into the existing extermination fleet. Even if their extermination fleet failed a paranoia check (because they are threatened), none of their home systems are threatened; so they keep the offense posture. They circle around, and transform their 100 CP worth of unidentified ships into 5 more battleships.
1. Round 1 Their Battleships shoot once more. They rolled 10,2,10,10,6,2 against our Type IXs (2 hits ≤5), followed by a 3 against our Type XIs (hit ≤3)
• Benefitting from FSB again, our Type XIs have rolled 2,3,4,4,5,5,5,6,7,8,8,8,9 (8 hits ≤6), taking out 2 battleships and damaging a third

• Round 2: Before their remaining 5 Battleships make a decision, they make another morale check. Although not as overwhelming as the previous battle (2 AI ships lost + 1 AI ship damaged + player FSB - 1 damaged player ship), their threshold is still a high 62. A 97 was rolled, allowing them to stay in this battle. They rolled 3,3,3,5,7 (3 hits ≤3)
• Our Type XIs have rolled 1,3,3,4,7,7,7,8,8,9,10 (4 hits ≤6), taking out another 2 battleships
• Round 3: Their remaining 3 Battleships conduct yet another morale check, and with the threshold back up to 91. An 87 was rolled. Just a bit too low to stay in the battle, so they retreat away

stages.heading: Stardate 2409.9 (The Battle for Sector E5)
commentary: Player Turn

We make a sweeping advance with the 12 Type XIs that survived the previous battle (while keeping the one Type IX screened). After their 3 battleships rolled 4,6,10 (0 hits ≤3), we rolled 2,3,4,5,6,8,8,8,9,10,10 (5 hits ≤6). But, since we only demolished one of them; they get to conduct a morale check, having rolled a 20 (versus their threshold of 50) and retreating away with their last 2 battleships.
Alien Turn

Their 2 battleships are still worth 40 CP, enough to avoid being redesignated as a shattered fleet. Althuogh paranoia checke are meaningless for extermination fleets, one is still necessary because it could influence their other fleet. A 76 was rolled (pass >62), allowing both fleets to retain offense posture.
After circling around, their ships rolled 1,2 (2 hits ≤3). Our remaining Type XIs then rolled 2,2,3,5,5,5,6,6,6,7 (9 hits ≤6), overkilling their fleet out of existence.

stages.heading: Stardate 2410.0
commentary: And thus begins our depletion process. Combined with the fact that one of our other colonies has been damaged, and we only produced 8 natural hulls this phase. We still buy an extra homeworld hull.
{prodTable}{replicatorTable}

Apollo's military has taken a huge hit, but it also reduces their maintenance. Its home system rolls are 2,3,5,5,8,8,8. Again, the only aspect where it makes sense to conduct a paranoia check is Exploration; having rolled a 35 (which now fails), causing them to buy Exploration 1. Their sole remaining fleet is threatened, so they rolled 1,2 for bid right away. Their paranoia tier is high enough they now net a +6 bonus, so they bid 7 CP. We made no attempt to deny the initiative, so they take it back.
For fleet launch, they rolled a 9. No launch takes place this time, so they instead build another FOB.

stages.heading: Stardate 2410.2
commentary: Since Apollo lost its extermination fleet in battle, their sole expansion fleet must make a paranoia check. A 46, which fails. Rather than engaging our fleet in battle; their fleet switches to defense posture, and falls back to the nebula.

stages.heading: Stardate 2410.5
commentary: Their fleet remains threatened still. A 55 was rolled for the next paranoia check, again a failure. Since further falling back would cause them to leave a choke point, they will not do that. Instead, they will hold their position.

We push into the nebula. The tech money they have is neither sufficient for a fleet strategy change, nor is it sufficient for Attack 2 or Defense 2. Thus, their unidentified fleet becomes 3 battleships. These ships then rolled 2,4,6 (1 hit ≤3). Our Type XIs then rolled 1,2,2,3,5,5,6,9,9,10 (7 hits ≤7), taking advantage of their inability to use their Defense equipment. They then perform a morale check; an 8, which easily fails. They retreat to one of their colonies.

stages.heading: Stardate 2410.8
commentary: With their choke point no longer defended, their home systems are now exposed. Their single battleship now constitutes a shattered fleet, so it will remain at the FOB. Since we just entered their home systems, they could defend with the fleet bank that has not already been spent. A 4 was rolled, so no stragglers were found.

stages.heading: Stardate 2411.0
commentary: Our depletion process resumes. We buy another homeworld hull with our remaining CP.
{prodTable}{replicatorTable}

Apollo has no fleets left to maintain. Its home system rolls are 2,2,2,4,6,7,7,8. As usual, the only aspect where it makes sense to conduct a paranoia check is Exploration; having rolled a 40 (failure), causing them to buy Exploration 2. Their home systems are threatened, so they rolled 1,10 for bid. Their paranoia is so high they now get a +7 bonus, so they bid 8 CP.

For fleet launch, they rolled a 7. They finally launch a fresh fleet. They are unable to upgrade their movement. Since their home systems are being invaded, they place this fleet exclusively at their homeworld.

stages.heading: Stardate 2411.1 (The Battle for Vortigern)
commentary: The invasion of Apollo's space continues. Despite having spent their fleet bank, they could draw from their tech bank to replace a shortage for random encounters. A 6 was rolled, so none appear anyway). Worse, their known battleship is much too slow to justify assisting the rest of their newest fleet with react move, an ability they just unlocked.

Once again, their tech bank is too low to change fleet strategy. The rest of the fleet then use their nanomachines to build 3 battleships and 1 battlecruiser, spending all of their assigned money. Taking advantage of their Tactics advantage; their ships then rolled 1,1,7,8 (2 hits ≤3). Our remaining Type XIs then rolled 3,10,9,8,9,4/1,4,5 (5 hits ≤6). Their ships then roll a 62 for morale, very barely failing the check and retreating to the homeworld.

We can now bombard their colonies. First, they rolled a 1, placing 2 Light Infantry. We then rolled 1,1,2,4,7,7,9,9,10 (6 hits ≤7), easily destroying their colony! Unlike Replicator Solitaire, we do not have to colonize the planet we just glassed, if we do not want to.

stages.heading: Stardate 2411.4A (The Battle for Vortigern)
commentary: Angry that one of their colonies was utterly glassed, Apollo is finally able to merge their [known] battleships, and conducts another battle against our fleet.
Their 3 Battleships rolled 1,9,9 (1 hit ≤3). Our Type XIs then rolled 1,1,2,2,2,4,6,6,10 (8 hits ≤6). Their last Battleship then rolled a 7, causing it to run back to the homeworld.

stages.heading: Stardate 2411.4P (The Battle for Altair)
commentary: This is it!! All that remains defending their homeworld is a single battleship, and their base; versus a fleet of 9 Type XIs. Their forces rolled 5/6 (1 hit ≤5/3). Our Type XIs then rolled 3,3,5,5,5,7,8,8,10 (5 hits ≤6). Their battleship then rolls a 46 for morale, chickening out of the battle yet again.
On the bombardment step, they rolled a 1 to recruit 1 light infantry. We thrn rolled 1,1,2,3,5,6,6,9,10 (7 hits ≤7), easily eliminating their homeworld!!

stages.heading: Stardate 2411.7 (Conclusion)
commentary: No need to worry about concession if we can just go for their throat... and end this playthrough on the spot by glassing their homeworld. Admittedly, we did make a deviation when we opted to give them an extra 10 CP, instead of an alien technology. We could not use them anyway, so we figured it is fitting to simplify that process. We likely would have not simplified this process if they had Terraforming 1+ as a goal.

It was also a bit awkward seeing the AP bot struggle regarding bombarding our colonies without access to Anti-Replicator transports. Yes, they managed to damage our economy just before depletion; but it was not very much damage (only 1 colony was damaged), and that we were already able to amass a large number of hulls (40 hulls worth at the time), not to mention we were able to wrestle the initiative away from them on what was probably the most pivotal set of turns.

We also wonder how AP bots should treat deep space if Heavy Terrain is in effect. Considering how dangerous the smaller sizes can be; it might be most effective to double the number of rows in every aspect; sans Movement, and before applying the Replicator modifier.

The rest of the decision making is tough to do when sticking it to the book is not always beneficial to them. We had to use the prime directive in one form anyway to get the AI out of an impossible situation by forcing a merge of fleets. If their second fleet launch had somehow failed, we would allow their expansion fleet to uncover a path beyond the barren planet as if it was an extermination fleet; taking advantage of the fleet bank that would instead be stockpiled to avoid being stuck behind a wall of unexplored terrain.
All in all, we probably will challenge Normal difficulty again (but possibly on a larger versus map), to further improve our understanding of this extremely sophisticated AI system.
